text:
University College Hospital

National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery

Clinic Date: 1 February 2012

Dr Susan
University College Hospital
National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery, London WC1N 3BG

Dear Dr Susan,

William Harris, DOB: 15-02-1965, Hospital No: H563829 NHS No. 9273645810
10 Meadow Park, London, EC1V 9BT

Summary/Diagnoses: History of seizures with prior traumatic brain injury; cognitive slowing reported by carers; osteoporosis; prior alcohol dependence now in remission; lives in supported accommodation; currently abstinent from alcohol; smoker. Religious fasting periods may alter routine.

Medication: Levetiracetam 1000 mg twice daily; Tegretol Retard 400 mg twice daily; Topiramate 75 mg morning and 100 mg evening; Vitamin B compound; Calcichew; Strontium sachets; Thiamine; Atorvastatin; Lansoprazole; Bisoprolol; Ramipril; Carbocisteine; Aqueous cream.

I reviewed him in clinic today, accompanied by his key worker from the residence. They report he has been engaging with day-centre activities and that mealtimes have been adjusted around religious observance. On fasting days he tends to delay his morning medications until later, and evening doses can be taken late after sunset. We discussed strategies to maintain dose timing within pragmatic windows to minimise variability.

Seizures: Over the past several months there has been some fluctuation, partially coinciding with disrupted sleep and delayed medication times during fasting periods. He had a cluster of three seizures in August (short, not full convulsions, fluctuating awareness, self-terminating). In October he had six nocturnal seizures, and in January a single tonic seizure was recorded during respite care. No injuries were reported. Carers are keeping a log and have been advised to note the relationship to sleep deprivation and dose timing.

Examination: He was alert, oriented to person and place, with mildly slowed processing speed. Gait steady with a single-point stick; no recent falls. Cardiovascular and respiratory examinations unremarkable. No focal new neurological deficit.

Plan:
- Continue current antiepileptic regimen without dose changes today.
- Reinforce adherence plan around fasting: aim to keep twice-daily dosing approximately 12 hours apart; if delayed, avoid missed doses and avoid dose stacking; carers to document actual times taken.
- Provide a simple written seizure action plan to residence staff; continue rescue protocol not indicated at present as events have been brief and self-terminating.
- Request serum biochemistry including electrolytes, liver function, vitamin D, and full blood count via GP; check bone health measures are up to date given osteoporosis history.
- Follow-up in 6 months, sooner if frequency increases or events evolve.

Yours sincerely,

He had a cluster of three seizures in August (short, not full convulsions, fluctuating awareness, self-terminating). In October he had six nocturnal seizures, and in January a single tonic seizure was recorded during respite care

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter provides explicit counts over recent months: a cluster of three seizures in August, six nocturnal seizures in October, and a single tonic seizure in January. These are discrete months with specific totals, summing to 10 seizures across three specified months within the recent several-month period. No broader time window is explicitly stated beyond these months; however, the events are clearly within a 6‑month window leading up to the clinic date (1 Feb 2012): August, October, and January are all within the preceding 6 months (Aug 2011–Jan 2012). Therefore, the best normalized frequency is 10 per 6 months. This is not seizure free as events occurred within the last 6 months. The highest frequency rule does not change this as the counts are additive for the same dimension.
